TopModule.demo_behavior:
Module: TopModule I would like you to implement a module named TopModule with the following interface. All input and output ports are one bit unless otherwise specified. - input  clk - input  load - input  data (256 bits) - output q (256 bits) The module should implemement a "game" played on a two-dimensional grid of cells, where each cell is either 1 (alive) or 0 (dead). At each time step, each cell changes state depending on how many neighbours it has: (1) 0-1 neighbour: Cell becomes 0. (2) 2 neighbours: Cell state does not change. (3) 3 neighbours: Cell becomes 1. (4) 4+ neighbours: Cell becomes 0. The game is formulated for an infinite grid. In this circuit, we will use a 16x16 grid. To make things more interesting, we will use a 16x16 toroid, where the sides wrap around to the other side of the grid. For example, the corner cell (0,0) has 8 neighbours: (15,1), (15,0), (15,15), (0,1), (0,15), (1,1), (1,0), and (1,15). The 16x16 grid is represented by a length 256 vector, where each row of 16 cells is represented by a sub-vector: q[15:0] is row 0, q[31:16] is row 1, etc. (1) load: Loads data into q at the next clock edge, for loading initial state. Active high synchronous. (2) q: The 16x16 current state of the game, updated every clock cycle. The game state should advance by one timestep every clock cycle. Assume all sequential logic is triggered on the positive edge of the clock.
Key rules: Implement behavior exactly as described in the prompt.
Reset: Only as explicitly described in the prompt.
Corner cases: No additional corner cases beyond prompt requirements.
Performance: Use prompt-defined timing/latency behavior.

test_goals: Pass benchmark harness checks for all provided tests.
verification.oracle_strategy: Use benchmark-provided reference outputs as oracle.
pass_fail_criteria: DUT outputs match oracle outputs for benchmark tests.
